paper.title_en: Neural-network-enhanced Bayesian global analysis of relativistic heavy-ion collisions
paper.abstract_en: We introduce a novel deep convolutional neural-network-(NN) enhanced Bayesian global analysis of bulk observables in highest-energy heavy-ion collisions using relativistic <math><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">D</mi></mrow></math> second-order viscous hydrodynamics with a dynamical freeze-out and with perturbative quantum chromodynamics (QCD) and saturation-based initial conditions from the event-by-event EKRT model. Our analysis has <math><mrow><mn>13</mn><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></math> free parameters for the QCD-matter properties <math><mo>+</mo></math> initial state, which are constrained by the experimental data from <math><mrow><msqrt><msub><mi>s</mi><mrow><mi>N</mi><mi>N</mi></mrow></msub></msqrt><mo>=</mo><mn>200</mn></mrow></math> GeV <math><mrow><mi>Au</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>Au</mi></mrow></math> collisions at Relativistic Heavy Ion Collider and 2.76 TeV <math><mrow><mi>Pb</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>Pb</mi></mrow></math>, 5.02 TeV <math><mrow><mi>Pb</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>Pb</mi></mrow></math>, and 5.44 TeV <math><mrow><mi>Xe</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>Xe</mi></mrow></math> collisions at the Large Hadron Collider. We replace the computationally demanding hydrodynamical simulations by NNs, which predict bulk observables directly from the initial energy density profiles, event by event, and account for the QCD-matter properties. With the NN output, we train the Gaussian process emulators for obtaining centrality-class averaged observables and their uncertainties. The NNs reduce the computing time significantly, enabling us to include also statistics-hungry flow observables like <math><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></math> and the normalized symmetric cumulant <math><mrow><mi>NSC</mi><mo>(</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>)</mo></mrow></math> in the analysis. In this paper, we demonstrate the feasibility of the NN-based Bayesian global analysis. We find the data favoring a specific shear viscosity <math><mrow><mi>η</mi><mo>/</mo><mi>s</mi></mrow></math> with a minimum-value plateau at temperatures <math><mrow><mn>150</mn><mo>≲</mo><mi>T</mi><mo>≲</mo><mn>230</mn></mrow></math> MeV, with <math><mrow><mn>0.12</mn><mo>≲</mo><msub><mrow><mo>(</mo><mi>η</mi><mo>/</mo><mi>s</mi><mo>)</mo></mrow><mi>min</mi></msub><mo>≲</mo><mn>0.18</mn></mrow></math>. The bulk viscous coefficient <math><mrow><mi>ζ</mi><mo>/</mo><mi>s</mi></mrow></math> is nonzero at <math><mrow><mn>200</mn><mo>≲</mo><mi>T</mi><mo>≲</mo><mn>300</mn></mrow></math> MeV. The Knudsen number at the freeze-out is <math><mrow><mn>0.8</mn><mo>–</mo><mn>2.3</mn></mrow></math>, while the ratio of the mean free path to the system size at freeze-out is in the range <math><mrow><mn>0.3</mn><mo>–</mo><mn>1.2</mn></mrow></math>, implying that the freeze-out indeed happens at the expected limit of the applicability of hydrodynamics.
